data.title: Norsk Hydro declares new force majeure on Qatar aluminum - Reuters
data.description:
Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) (NHYKF) has declared a second force majeure on aluminum sales from Qatar after its Qatalum joint venture unexpectedly ended a marketing ​agreement with the Norwegian company, Reuters ‌reported Friday.

Norsk Hydro (NHYDY) (NHYKF), which owns 50% in the 648K metric tons/year Qatalum project alongside Qatar Aluminum Manufacturing, issued a first force majeure three months ago after ​the outbreak of the Iran war interrupted the plant's gas ​supply and Qatalum initiated a shutdown.

That force majeure remains in place even after Qatalum subsequently received enough gas ​to operate at 60% of capacity, but a second force majeure reportedly was issued to Hydro's (NHYDY) (NHYKF) customers on Friday related to its commercial arrangements with Qatalum.

"Despite Hydro's efforts to make Qatalum withdraw its ‌notice ⁠of termination, Qatalum has refused to do so and Qatalum has informed Hydro that it will not deliver metal under the relevant agreements," the force majeure notice reportedly said.

It was unclear why Qatalum had terminated the agreement, the report said.

data.title: DexCom's OTC CGM Stelo gains pediatric approval
data.description:
• The US FDA has approved DexCom's (DXCM) over-the-counter continuous glucose monitor Stelo for use in children two years and older.
  • The indication is for children who receive oral medication for diabetes.
  • Stelo consists of a wearable sensor that is paired to a smartphone app that tracks and provides glucose data every 15 minutes. The sensor is replaced every 15 days.
  • Stelo is the first OTC CGM approved for children.

data.title: Warner Bros. gains on report DOJ approves $111B sale to Paramount Skydance
data.description:
Warner Bros. (WBD) rose 0.6% in after-hours trading on a report that the Justice Department's antitrust division has approved its planned $111 billion sale to Paramount Skydance (PSKY).  

The decision is expected to be announced on Friday, according to a Politico report on Friday, which cited two people familiar with the matter. The regulator approved the deal without requiring any divestitures, behavioral remedies, or concessions. 

The DOJ approval comes after a Reuters report a week ago that several state attorneys plan to file a lawsuit to block  its acquisition by Paramount.

A potential lawsuit by state attorneys general would present another roadblock to regulatory approval, joining the European Union and FCC, who have voiced concerns about the merger. Last Tuesday, UK regulators opened an investigation into the deal, setting an August 7 deadline on whether to initiate a more intensive Phase 3 probe.

data.title: SpaceX gains nearly 20% in historic IPO as Elon Musk becomes world's first trillionaire
data.description:
We have liftoff.

SpaceX's (SPCX) long-awaited initial public offering happened on Friday, as shares officially opened on the Nasdaq at $150, for a gain of 11%. Shares ended the day at $160.95, up 19.2%, after touching a high of $176.52 earlier in the session. 

Musk and AI

The offering makes Elon Musk, who leads both SpaceX and Tesla (TSLA), the world's first trillionaire. Tesla shares fell modestly following SpaceX's debut.

That his net worth crossed the trillion-dollar mark after the debut is notable, given that much of his wealth is tied to the rocket and artificial intelligence company. His stake in SpaceX is worth roughly $866B, and he retained overwhelming voting control after the IPO, giving him effective authority over SpaceX's strategic direction and board composition.

According to a new filing, Musk holds options to purchase 350M Class B common shares at an exercise price of $8.3998 per share through Feb. 11, 2031. The Elon Musk Revocable Trust indirectly owns 526.2M Class A shares. Another vehicle, EM 2024 GRAT-A, indirectly owns 7.4M Class A shares.

The offering was led by Goldman Sachs, Morgan Stanley, Bank of America, Citigroup, and JPMorgan Chase, alongside a broader syndicate of banks. 

Goldman Sachs President John Waldron appeared on Bloomberg on Friday and said the offering was indicative of investor demand for funding all things related to artificial intelligence.

Recently, SpaceX has focused more on artificial intelligence than just putting rockets into space. Musk has talked about putting data centers into space, using the company's rockets to get them there. The company has other AI-related assets, including its Grok chatbot and its Colossus data centers.

It has also increasingly positioned itself as an AI infrastructure provider, providing computing power to Anthropic (ANTHRO) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) in separate deals, aside from its traditional businesses in launch services and satellite communications. Goldman Sachs recently said it expects SpaceX's revenue related to AI to jump 100 times between now and 2030.

Google's parent company, Alphabet, is a long-time investor in SpaceX.

Unusual nature 

In an unusual move, SpaceX priced 555.5M shares at $135 apiece, instead of allowing the book runners to determine the price based on supply and demand.

Another sign of the unusual nature of the offering is the heavy allocation for retail investors. Approximately 20% of the offering was granted to retail investors, a much higher percentage than most IPOs. Interest from retail investors reportedly topped $100B, including offers from outside the U.S.

Asset manager BlackRock (BLK) reportedly requested at least $5B worth of SpaceX shares, indicating heavy demand from institutional investors. In total, more than $350B in orders were placed, including $250B from institutional investors, Bloomberg reported.

For comparison purposes, artificial intelligence chipmaker Cerebras (CBS), which went public last month, increased its share price multiple times before the IPO, and only a few retail investors were granted an allocation, according to complaints on social media.

In total, SpaceX raised $75B in the offering, which was reportedly oversubscribed by a factor of four.

Another sign of the unusual nature of the offering was that several Wall Street investment firms have already started coverage on SpaceX. Earlier this week, Oppenheimer put an Outperform rating and a $190 price target on SpaceX, arguing the Elon Musk-led company is uniquely positioned to capitalize on the convergence of artificial intelligence, communications infrastructure, and space-based computing. 

Wedbush Securities said the offering is reflective of “an inflection” of space and artificial intelligence. “Overall, SpaceX going public is an important moment for the broader tech sector in our view as this AI Revolution and data takes this next step forward,” the research firm wrote in a note to clients.

Wedbush also maintained its position that the SpaceX offering will eventually lead to a merger with Tesla, noting that Tesla already owns a stake in SpaceX via its merger with xAI, which occurred earlier this year. 

“Musk wants to own and control more of the AI ecosystem and step by step the holy grail could be combining SpaceX and Tesla in some way to give the connected tissue between both disruptive tech stalwarts looking to lead the AI Revolution in this next tech chapter for the market,” analysts at the research firm wrote.

Changing the status quo

Adding on to the unusual nature of the IPO is that SpaceX will become eligible for inclusion in certain indexes, such as those provided by the Nasdaq (NDAQ) and FTSE Russell, much faster than previous offerings.

The speed of the inclusion has prompted some concern from investment officials from several large public pension systems in the U.S. They have asked the major index providers to justify the recent rule changes.

Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D.-MA) asked the Securities and Exchange Commission earlier this week to delay the offering. She argued the blockbuster offering posed unusual risks to retail investors, due to its valuation, governance structure, and expected inclusion in major stock indexes.

Despite the aforementioned concerns, many Americans already have some exposure to SpaceX, via ETFs owned by Cathie Wood’s Ark Invest or mutual funds owned by Fidelity Investment or Baron Funds. Venture capital firms Founders Fund and Andreessen Horowitz also own significant stakes in SpaceX.

Shattering records

With an initial valuation of $1.75T, SpaceX is the most valuable company ever to go public.

Alibaba (BABA) went public in September 2015 at a valuation of $169.4B, while Meta Platforms (META) (then known as Facebook) was valued at $81.3B in May 2012. Uber (UBER) was valued at $75.5B in May 2019. 

Only a handful of companies have ever even reached the rarified air of a valuation of $1T or more. Excluding Saudi Aramco, which does not trade in the U.S., the group is limited to names such as Nvidia (NVDA), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Tesla (TSLA), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and Walmart (WMT). Most of these names took decades to achieve such a financial feat.

(This story has been updated with the latest pricing information.)

data.title: Xbox wants to go all in on top-tier franchises; weighs possible spin-out: report
data.description:
Microsoft's (MSFT) Xbox intends to focus on developing new titles for its most popular gaming franchises, such as Halo, Elder Scrolls, and Fallout, according to The Information.

What's more, Microsoft has considered spinning out its Xbox division or restructuring the unit as a wholly owned subsidiary, the report said, citing people familiar with the discussions. However, there are no plans to take such measures at this time. Microsoft already operates LinkedIn and GitHub as wholly owned subsidiaries.

Xbox is undergoing a sweeping reorganization of the business under new CEO Asha Sharma. The division plans to "significantly slash" budgets for marketing and other areas amid a "resetting of the business." This was revealed in an internal memo on Wednesday that Sharma shared publicly.  

"Our current platform infrastructure is not built for the battle ahead," Sharma said in the memo. "Our systems are overly complex, spanning hundreds of dependencies, which hinders our ability to move fast. We've become too reliant on vendors to operate our systems and must become more self-reliant as an engineering culture to build for the future. We must increase the value we ship to players while decreasing the time it takes to do so. Going forward, we'll evolve and rebuild our stack and look at capabilities across all of XBOX and potential M&A to help us win in hardware, PC, mobile, and streaming."

Part of this plan is to regularly develop new titles for its most popular franchises, the report said. This includes new titles for Fallout and Elder Scrolls, which have not had new mainline titles in more than a decade. Sharma also wants to increase its spending on game development for the upcoming fiscal year. She also would like to develop new Halo games at a faster clip, as Xbox hasn't published a major Halo title in five years.

data.title: S&P’s decision not to include SpaceX is a huge mistake – TD Securities’ Peter Haynes
data.description:
S&P Global's decision to exclude SpaceX (SPCX) from the S&P 500 index (SP500), (SPY) is a significant mistake that forces the benchmark provider into making a risky bet against one of the world's most valuable companies, according to Peter Haynes, head of index and market structure research at TD Securities.

In an interview with CNBC, Haynes expressed his disappointment with S&P's choice not to follow through on a consultation that suggested mega-cap companies like SpaceX (SPCX) would become eligible for index inclusion within six months of their IPO.

Instead, the waiting period extends to at least one year, compounded by a profitability requirement that could keep high-valuation companies on the sidelines indefinitely.

"S&P is making a huge bet against what is arguably very valuable companies," Haynes said. "The market is pricing these stocks, whether you agree or not, at trillion-dollar companies and not including them in the primary benchmarks that cover that segment of the market for an extended period of time is, in my opinion, not the right decision."

Haynes argued that the fundamental purpose of an index is to represent the market and its segments. While SpaceX will be included in almost every other important U.S. benchmark, its absence from the S&P 500 creates a notable gap in what he called "the most important bellwether benchmark in the U.S."

The TD Securities analyst also challenged the profitability requirement, noting that numerous companies already in S&P's 1,500 indices are currently unprofitable despite having been profitable at the time of their inclusion.

"I do think that profit test is a little bit outdated," Haynes said.

Drawing a historical parallel, Haynes pointed to Microsoft (MSFT), which took eight to 10 years to gain entry into the S&P 500 (SP500) in the 1990s. He questioned whether that prolonged exclusion would be viewed favorably in hindsight, suggesting the current approach represents a similar gamble.

While acknowledging S&P's right to enforce its rules, Haynes emphasized that the index provider is functioning as "arguably the largest active manager in the world" by making subjective decisions about which companies qualify for inclusion.

"I respect the fact that they are going to stick to their rules," he said. "I don't agree with it because I think they're making a big bet."

S&P 500 ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Beyond SpaceX: The defense-space convergence
data.description:
Investors Should Know: With the rollout of the SpaceX (SPCX) IPO, investors have become more aware of the potential of space-focused innovation. However, there has long been a convergence between this technology and the defense industry. Areas like missile defense, satellite-based early warning, and multi-orbit communications represent potential investment opportunities within aerospace suppliers, satellite operators, and RF semiconductor makers.

Background

The defense-space convergence theme reflects a shift in how modern military systems are built and connected. Missile defense, reconnaissance, and battlefield communications increasingly depend on space-based infrastructure, from low-Earth orbit satellite constellations to advanced radio frequency components that link ground systems to multiple orbital layers.

The sector includes traditional defense hardware like missile propulsion, interceptors, and related subsystems. Beyond that, there are opportunities in  space-based sensing and early warning, where satellite operators are partnering directly with weapons-system integrators. Meanwhile, in the RF semiconductor space, beamforming chips enable the always-on, multi-orbit connectivity that defense users increasingly require.

Stocks to Watch

Key publicly traded names in this space include RTX Corporation (RTX) and L3Harris Technologies (LHX), both large defense contractors with missile systems and aerospace electronics businesses. TTM Technologies (TTMI), ATI Inc. (ATI), and V2X, Inc. (VVX) also carry exposure through defense manufacturing and mission-support operations. On the satellite side, Spire Global (SPIR) has positioned its LEO constellation as part of sovereign defense infrastructure. Sivers Semiconductors (SIVR) supplies Ka-band beamforming integrated circuits used in multi-orbit satellite terminals.

Additional aerospace and defense names with relevant industry exposure include AAR Corp. (AIR), Mercury Systems (MRCY), Astronics Corporation (ATRO), Bombardier Inc. (BDRBF), AerSale Corporation (ASLE), Innovative Aerosystems (ISSC), Ondas Inc. (ONDS), and Ducommun Incorporated (DCO).

Key Takeaways

  • The completion of the SpaceX IPO has put a spotlight on space-related technology, a segment that has already seen significant innovation within the broader defense industry.
  • Defense and space systems are converging around missile defense and early warning, with satellite operators now partnering directly with weapons-system integrators.
  • RF beamforming semiconductors are emerging as a critical enabling layer, supporting resilient multi-orbit satellite communications for defense users across LEO, MEO, and GEO.
  • Large defense contractors are expanding missile-systems manufacturing capacity, with factory modernization tied explicitly to homeland defense interceptors and industrial-base speed objectives.
  • The convergence theme spans multiple supply-chain tiers — from prime contractors and satellite operators to component-level semiconductor suppliers — giving investors several distinct entry points.

News Roundup

Recent developments across the defense-space supply chain illustrate how the convergence theme is playing out in practice. Missile-defense capacity expansion, satellite-based early warning partnerships, and RF component orders each reflect a different layer of the same structural shift.

  • SpaceX (SPCX) — The rocket and spacecraft maker completed its long-awaited IPO, pricing its initial public offering at $135 per share.  The company raised $75B in the offering, with a market cap near $1.8T.
  • L3Harris Technologies (LHX) — The company's Missile Solutions business spans propulsion, maneuvering, seekers, and launch technologies, according to its recent strategic discussion. Management described factory expansion and modernization tied directly to homeland defense interceptors and industrial-base speed and scale objectives.
  • Spire Global (SPIR) — Spire signed a memorandum of understanding with Diehl Defence to explore satellite-based intelligence and early warning against ballistic and hypersonic missiles. The agreement combines Spire's constellation operations with Diehl's air-defense and guided missile systems integration, with Diehl framing the goal as "connecting intelligence from space to weapons systems and command centers."
  • Sivers Semiconductors (SIVR) — ALL.SPACE awarded Sivers an $8.2 million production order for Ka-band beamforming integrated circuits. The announcement cited accelerating demand for multi-orbit satellite communications across LEO, MEO, and GEO, and highlighted defense organizations' need for always-on connectivity across orbital regimes.

Defense-Space Convergence Stocks in Focus

The screener below identifies publicly traded companies aligned with the unmanned drones and robotics and global defense and aerospace themes, filtered for Strong Buy or Buy Quant Ratings and ranked from highest to lowest rating.

data.title: ONE Gas initiated Buy at BTIG, driven by large load growth opportunities
data.description:
ONE Gas (OGS) up 2.6% in Friday's trading as BTIG Research launches coverage of the natural gas utility with a Buy rating and $93 price target, saying growing demand from large load customers seeking natural gas for speedy baseload and backup power generation creates a unique growth opportunity for the company while still maintaining defensiveness through its traditional infrastructural investments.

BTIG analyst Alex Kania views the company's 5%-7% long-term earnings per share growth rate as in line with other electric utilities and gas local distribution companies; coupled with the stock's current discount valuation, he sees value "given its track record of execution, solid regulatory jurisdictions and location on the map, and large load opportunity set."

ONE Gas (OGS) has a history of meeting or exceeding guidance and delivering 7% EPS growth over the last 10 years, and given its large load projects and traditional gas LDC growth as well as its conservative approach in projecting its outlook, Kania believes the company is positioned well to continue delivering on its plans.

data.title: Roku jumps on report of sale talks
data.description:
Roku Inc. (ROKU) soared 20% on a report that the streaming video platform is in discussions to sell itself.

Roku (ROKU) has been in talks with at least one US media company about a potential deal, according to a Bloomberg report on Friday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

No final decisions have been made on a possible sale, and there's no certainty that any deal will happen, according to the report.

Roku didn't immediately respond to Bloomberg's request for comment.

data.title: Robinhood customers face issues in first minutes of SpaceX trading - report
data.description:
Robinhood Markets (HOOD) (HOOD:CA) customers are said to have faced issues during the first minutes of SpaceX (SPCX) trading today.

Roughly 5,000 outages were reported at Robinhood as of 11:58 AM ET in New York, with some customers experiencing latency and intermittent issues, Bloomberg News reported on Friday, citing the website Downdetector and a company spokesperson.

The issues reportedly receded by 12:30 PM ET.

The company saw a record-breaking traffic today, HOOD said in an emailed statement to Bloomberg.

SPCX opened ~11% higher at $150 today after pricing 555.56M shares at $135 apiece. The stock was trading 25.56% higher at $169.50 during afternoon trading.

Robinhood, alongside Charles Schwab (SCHW), Fidelity Investments, SoFi Technologies (SOFI), and Morgan Stanley's (MS) E*Trade, was offering SpaceX shares to retail customers. However, other platforms didn’t see significant issues, Downdetector reportedly said.
